1.outcome: Read and write Luau expressions, decisions, and loops without guessing.

proof.title: Interactive gameplay rule
proof.brief: Prove the fundamentals inside Roblox Studio with one small, observable system.
checks: Create a Script that stores at least two local values with clear names.
Use a conditional to change an Instance property from those values.
Use a bounded loop and confirm the Output has no warnings or infinite yields.

2.outcome: Build functions and table APIs with intentional scope and state.

proof.title: Reusable inventory module
proof.brief: Build a ModuleScript whose state cannot be accidentally overwritten by callers.
checks: Create a ModuleScript with add, remove, and get functions.
Keep the backing table local and return copies or controlled results.
Require it from a second Script and test success plus failure cases.

3.outcome: Transform strings, tables, numbers, buffers, and coroutines with the right tool.

proof.title: Utility pipeline
proof.brief: Turn raw gameplay data into a safe, formatted result using standard libraries.
checks: Normalize and format a player-facing string from raw input.
Process a table as a queue without leaving invalid indices.
Clamp a gameplay value and print tests for its minimum and maximum boundaries.

4.outcome: Use strict types to make invalid game states difficult to represent.

proof.title: Strict inventory model
proof.brief: Use Luau's type system to document and defend a real data boundary.
checks: Enable --!strict and define exported item plus inventory types.
Represent at least one optional or union state explicitly.
Remove all type errors without replacing useful types with any.

5.outcome: Explain bytecode, registers, allocation, and optimization without cargo culting.

proof.title: Evidence-led performance pass
proof.brief: Measure one hot path, change it, and preserve the evidence rather than guessing.
checks: Capture a baseline in Studio's MicroProfiler or Script Performance view.
Change one allocation, bridge access, or repeated lookup in the measured path.
Re-measure and write one sentence explaining whether the change helped.

6.outcome: Coordinate Instances, tasks, signals, and engine boundaries safely.

proof.title: Lifecycle-safe signal controller
proof.brief: Build an engine-facing controller that cleans up every connection it creates.
checks: Connect an Instance or RunService signal through one controller module.
Store and disconnect every connection when the controller is destroyed.
Run the lifecycle twice and confirm callbacks do not duplicate.

7.outcome: Replicate, validate, persist, and query world state from the correct side.

proof.title: Two-player authoritative action
proof.brief: Prove a client request can be useful without letting the client own the truth.
checks: Send one player action through a RemoteEvent in a two-player Studio test.
Validate type, range, ownership, and rate on the server before mutation.
Show the accepted result to both clients and reject one intentionally invalid request.

8.outcome: Use parallel boundaries and security identities without sharing unsafe state.

proof.title: Parallel safety audit
proof.brief: Move a suitable workload behind an Actor boundary and document its trust model.
checks: Choose a workload with no unsafe shared Instance mutation.
Exchange only explicit, serializable data across the boundary.
Document one exploit or race the architecture prevents.

9.outcome: Build Roblox UI that responds, animates, and remains usable across devices.

proof.title: Responsive shop HUD
proof.brief: Build one real HUD surface and validate it on desktop plus a mobile emulator.
checks: Use layout and scale constraints instead of one fixed screen size.
Wire an input event and a state-driven tween with a clear disabled state.
Test desktop and mobile emulation with no clipped controls or unsafe touch target.

10.outcome: Combine architecture, persistence, remotes, UI, cleanup, and testing into a playable slice.

proof.title: Playable tycoon vertical slice
proof.brief: Complete the smallest version that proves the whole system works together.
checks: A player can claim a plot, buy an upgrade, and see authoritative state change.
State survives rejoin and invalid remote requests do not change the economy.
The HUD reflects server state and all runtime connections clean up correctly.

exercise:1:0.name: Variable Binding
exercise:1:0.prompt: Create a local variable named 'limit' with value 100, and a local variable named 'name' with value 'Roblox'.
exercise:1:0.harness:
assert(limit == 100, 'limit must be exactly 100')
assert(name == 'Roblox', 'name must be exactly "Roblox"')

exercise:1:1.name: Arithmetic & Precedence
exercise:1:1.prompt: Compute the average of three numbers 'a', 'b', and 'c' and store it in a local variable 'average' using parentheses to ensure correct precedence.
exercise:1:1.starter:
local a, b, c = 10, 20, 30
local average = a + b + c / 3 -- Fix this expression!
exercise:1:1.harness: assert(average == 20, 'average must be computed using correct parentheses precedence, got: ' .. tostring(average))

exercise:1:2.name: Logical Operators
exercise:1:2.prompt: Complete the function 'canEnterLevel(level, isPro, badgeCount)' to return true if the player is Pro OR has at least 5 badges, AND the level is not gated (level <= 10).
exercise:1:2.starter:
local function canEnterLevel(level: number, isPro: boolean, badgeCount: number): boolean
    return false
end
exercise:1:2.harness:
assert(canEnterLevel(5, true, 2) == true, 'Pro should access level 5')
assert(canEnterLevel(5, false, 6) == true, 'Non-Pro with 6 badges should access level 5')
assert(canEnterLevel(5, false, 2) == false, 'Non-Pro with 2 badges should be denied')
assert(canEnterLevel(12, true, 10) == false, 'Pro should be denied access to level 12 (gated)')

exercise:1:3.name: While Loop Accumulator
exercise:1:3.prompt: Write a function 'sumNumbers(n)' that uses a while loop to sum all integers from 1 up to 'n' (inclusive) and return the result.
exercise:1:3.starter:
local function sumNumbers(n: number): number
    return 0
end
exercise:1:3.harness:
assert(sumNumbers(5) == 15, 'sum of 1..5 must be 15')
assert(sumNumbers(1) == 1, 'sum of 1..1 must be 1')
assert(sumNumbers(10) == 55, 'sum of 1..10 must be 55')

exercise:2:0.name: Multiple Returns
exercise:2:0.prompt: Complete the function 'minMax(t)' to return both the minimum and maximum values in an array of numbers (in that order).
exercise:2:0.starter:
local function minMax(t: { number }): (number, number)
    return 0, 0
end
exercise:2:0.harness:
local min, max = minMax({5, 1, 9, 3})
assert(min == 1, 'min must be 1')
assert(max == 9, 'max must be 9')
local min2, max2 = minMax({-10, 20, 0})
assert(min2 == -10, 'min must be -10')
assert(max2 == 20, 'max must be 20')

exercise:2:1.name: Lexical Scope (Closure)
exercise:2:1.prompt: Write a function 'createCounter(startVal)' that returns a closure. Each time the closure is called, it increments the internal counter value by 1 and returns the new value.
exercise:2:1.starter:
local function createCounter(startVal: number): () -> number
    return function()
        return 0
    end
end
exercise:2:1.harness:
local c = createCounter(10)
assert(c() == 11, 'first call should yield 11')
assert(c() == 12, 'second call should yield 12')

exercise:2:2.name: Array Filtering
exercise:2:2.prompt: Write a function 'filterPositive(arr)' that returns a new array containing only elements from 'arr' that are strictly greater than 0.
exercise:2:2.harness:
local res = filterPositive({ -2, 5, 0, 10, -5 })
assert(#res == 2, 'should contain exactly 2 elements')
assert(res[1] == 5 and res[2] == 10, 'should be {5, 10}')
local res2 = filterPositive({ -1, -3 })
assert(#res2 == 0, 'should contain 0 elements for all negative inputs')

exercise:2:3.name: Dictionary Lookup
exercise:2:3.prompt: Write a function 'getItemPrice(inventory, itemName)' that returns the price of 'itemName' in the 'inventory' dictionary, or 0 if it doesn't exist.
exercise:2:3.starter:
local function getItemPrice(inventory: { [string]: number }, itemName: string): number
    return 0
end
exercise:2:3.harness:
local shop = { Sword = 100, Shield = 50 }
assert(getItemPrice(shop, 'Sword') == 100, 'Sword price should be 100')
assert(getItemPrice(shop, 'Potion') == 0, 'Potion price should be 0')
assert(getItemPrice({ Axe = 75 }, 'Axe') == 75, 'Axe price should be 75')

exercise:3:0.name: String Substrings
exercise:3:0.prompt: Extract the username portion from an email address (everything before the '@' symbol) using string.find and string.sub.
exercise:3:0.starter:
local function getUsername(email: string): string
    return ""
end
exercise:3:0.harness:
assert(getUsername('alex@luaux.com') == 'alex', 'username should be alex')
assert(getUsername('user1_test@domain.org') == 'user1_test', 'username should be user1_test')

exercise:3:1.name: String Formatting
exercise:3:1.prompt: Format a score display into a zero-padded 6-digit score using string.format (e.g. 123 -> 'Score: 000123').
exercise:3:1.starter:
local function formatScore(score: number): string
    return ""
end
exercise:3:1.harness:
assert(formatScore(123) == 'Score: 000123', 'should pad to 6 digits')
assert(formatScore(0) == 'Score: 000000', 'should pad zero to 6 digits')

exercise:3:2.name: Table Queue Operations
exercise:3:2.prompt: Maintain a queue array of player names. Implement enqueue(queue, name) (adds to end) and dequeue(queue) (removes and returns from front) using standard table methods.
exercise:3:2.starter:
local function enqueue(queue: { string }, name: string)
    -- Insert code here
end

local function dequeue(queue: { string }): string
    return ""
end
exercise:3:2.harness:
local q = {}
enqueue(q, 'Alex')
enqueue(q, 'Bob')
assert(q[1] == 'Alex' and q[2] == 'Bob', 'enqueue order is incorrect')
local name = dequeue(q)
assert(name == 'Alex', 'dequeue should return the first item')
assert(#q == 1 and q[1] == 'Bob', 'dequeue should shift elements left')

exercise:3:3.name: Math Clamp Health
exercise:3:3.prompt: Implement a function 'clampHealth(hp)' that limits character health between a minimum of 0 and a maximum of 100 using math.clamp.
exercise:3:3.starter:
local function clampHealth(hp: number): number
    return hp
end
exercise:3:3.harness:
assert(clampHealth(120) == 100, '120 should clamp to 100')
assert(clampHealth(-10) == 0, '-10 should clamp to 0')
assert(clampHealth(55) == 55, '55 should stay 55')

exercise:4:0.name: Explicit Table Types
exercise:4:0.prompt: Define a type alias 'Point' with coordinates 'x' and 'y' as numbers. Complete the function 'dist(p1: Point, p2: Point): number' to return the Euclidean distance between them.
exercise:4:0.starter:
-- Define type Point here

local function dist(p1, p2): number
    return 0
end

exercise:4:1.name: Union Types
exercise:4:1.prompt: Define a function 'toString(val)' that accepts a parameter of type 'string | number' and returns its string representation.
exercise:4:1.starter:
local function toString(val: string | number): string
    return ""
end
exercise:4:1.harness:
assert(toString(123) == '123', 'should format numbers')
assert(toString('test') == 'test', 'should return strings')

exercise:4:2.name: Optional Parameters
exercise:4:2.prompt: Write a function 'greet(name: string, prefix: string?)' that returns 'Hello, [prefix] [name]' if a prefix is provided, or 'Hello, [name]' if omitted.
exercise:4:2.starter:
local function greet(name: string, prefix: string?): string
    return ""
end
exercise:4:2.harness:
assert(greet('John') == 'Hello, John', 'should greet without prefix')
assert(greet('John', 'Mr.') == 'Hello, Mr. John', 'should greet with prefix')

exercise:4:3.name: Generics Identity Wrapper
exercise:4:3.prompt: Write a generic function 'wrap<T>(value: T): { data: T }' that wraps any input in a table under the 'data' key.
exercise:4:3.starter:
local function wrap(value)
    return value -- TODO: wrap this in a { data = ... } table instead
end
exercise:4:3.harness:
local w1 = wrap(42)
local w2 = wrap('test')
assert(w1.data == 42, 'should wrap number')
assert(w2.data == 'test', 'should wrap string')

exercise:5:0.name: GC Memory Tracking
exercise:5:0.prompt: Write measureAllocation() that records gcinfo(), allocates and retains 50,000 small tables through the second measurement, then returns the observed difference in KB. Treat the number as a noisy observation, not a guaranteed benchmark.
exercise:5:0.starter:
local function measureAllocation(): number
    local before = gcinfo()
    local retained = {}
    -- Allocate 50,000 tables into retained, then measure while they are still reachable
    return 0
end
exercise:5:0.harness:
local diff = measureAllocation()
assert(type(diff) == 'number', 'measurement must return a number')
assert(diff > 0, 'retained allocations should increase the observed memory reading, got: ' .. tostring(diff))

exercise:5:1.name: String Equality Semantics
exercise:5:1.prompt: Complete hasSameText(s1, s2) using Luau's equality operator. The language lets code observe equal string contents; internal interning is an implementation detail and this exercise deliberately does not claim to expose memory addresses.
exercise:5:1.starter:
local function hasSameText(s1: string, s2: string): boolean
    return false
end
exercise:5:1.harness:
local a = 'hello' .. ' world'
local b = 'hello world'
assert(hasSameText(a, b) == true, 'matching string contents must compare equal')
assert(hasSameText('hello', 'Hello') == false, 'string equality is case-sensitive')

exercise:5:2.name: Builtin Call Loop
exercise:5:2.prompt: Complete sumSines(count) by calling math.sin for each integer from 1 through count. This runtime test proves behavior only; inspecting compiler output is required before making claims about a particular FASTCALL opcode.
exercise:5:2.starter:
local function sumSines(count: number): number
    local sum = 0
    return sum
end
exercise:5:2.harness:
assert(sumSines(0) == 0, 'sum of 0 should be 0')
assert(sumSines(2) == math.sin(1) + math.sin(2), 'sum should match loop sines')

exercise:5:3.name: Table Resizing Optimization
exercise:5:3.prompt: Complete buildPreallocatedArray(n) with table.create(n), then fill indices 1 through n. Preallocation reserves array storage up front; measure a real workload before treating it as a meaningful optimization.
exercise:5:3.starter:
local function buildPreallocatedArray(n: number): { number }
    local t = {} -- TODO: pre-allocate with table.create(n), then fill 1..n
    return t
end
exercise:5:3.harness:
local t = buildPreallocatedArray(5000)
assert(#t == 5000, 'table size must be 5000')
assert(t[5000] == 5000, 'last item value must be 5000')

exercise:6:0.name: Boundary Value Snapshot
exercise:6:0.prompt: Model an engine-boundary read in pure Luau: cache part.Position.Y once before a 1,000-iteration loop, then sum the snapshot. In Studio, measure before assuming that fewer property reads materially improve performance.
exercise:6:0.starter:
local function sumPositions(part: any): number
    -- TODO: cache part.Position.Y in a local, then sum it 1,000 times
    return 0
end
exercise:6:0.harness:
local part = { Position = { Y = 5 } }
assert(sumPositions(part) == 5000, 'cached Y=5 summed 1,000 times must be 5,000')
part.Position.Y = 2
assert(sumPositions(part) == 2000, 'the next call must snapshot the new Y value')

exercise:6:1.name: Static Closure References
exercise:6:1.prompt: Optimize 'processItems(items)' to use a static helper function declared outside the loop instead of creating inline anonymous closures.
exercise:6:1.starter:
local processItem -- TODO: declare a static helper function here

local function processItems(items: { any })
    for _, item in ipairs(items) do
        -- TODO: call the static helper to increment item.Y by 1
    end
end

exercise:6:2.name: Deferred Execution Queue
exercise:6:2.prompt: The engine's task scheduler queues deferred callbacks and flushes them at defined resumption points. Model the mechanism: implement schedule(fn) that queues callbacks WITHOUT running them, and flush() that runs all queued callbacks in FIFO order, then clears the queue.
exercise:6:2.starter:
local queue = {}

local function schedule(fn: () -> ())
    -- TODO: add fn to the queue (do NOT call it here)
end

local function flush()
    -- TODO: run every queued callback in order, then clear the queue
end
exercise:6:2.harness:
local order = {}
schedule(function() table.insert(order, 1) end)
schedule(function() table.insert(order, 2) end)
assert(#order == 0, 'callbacks must NOT run when scheduled - only on flush')
flush()
assert(#order == 2 and order[1] == 1 and order[2] == 2, 'flush must run callbacks in FIFO order')
flush()
assert(#order == 2, 'flush must clear the queue - callbacks run only once')

exercise:6:3.name: Boundary Payload Guard
exercise:6:3.prompt: Implement safeSetPosition(part, pos) in pure Luau. Accept only a table containing numeric X, Y, and Z fields; return true and assign it when valid, otherwise return false without changing part.Position. Roblox Vector3 validation in Studio would use typeof(pos) == 'Vector3'.
exercise:6:3.starter:
local function safeSetPosition(part: any, pos: any): boolean
    -- Validate this sandbox's {X, Y, Z} payload before assigning it
    return false
end
exercise:6:3.harness:
local p = { Position = { X = 0, Y = 0, Z = 0 } }
assert(safeSetPosition(p, { X = 1, Y = 2, Z = 3 }) == true, 'valid numeric vector payload should be accepted')
assert(p.Position.X == 1 and p.Position.Y == 2 and p.Position.Z == 3, 'valid payload should write all coordinates')
assert(safeSetPosition(p, 'invalid') == false, 'non-table payload must be rejected')
assert(safeSetPosition(p, { X = 9, Y = 'bad', Z = 9 }) == false, 'every coordinate must be numeric')
assert(p.Position.X == 1, 'rejected payload must not mutate the prior position')

exercise:7:0.name: Delta Compression Packet
exercise:7:0.prompt: Construct a delta compression packet. Given an old state and a new state dictionary, return a new dictionary containing only the modified key-value pairs.
exercise:7:0.harness:
local old = { hp = 100, gold = 50 }
local new = { hp = 95, gold = 50 }
local delta = getDelta(old, new)
assert(delta.hp == 95, 'hp changed to 95')
assert(delta.gold == nil, 'gold did not change and should be compressed out')
local delta2 = getDelta({ x = 1 }, { x = 1, y = 2 })
assert(delta2.y == 2, 'added fields must be included')

exercise:7:1.name: Client Rate Limiter
exercise:7:1.prompt: Implement allowRequest(playerId, timestamp) to return false if the player's last request occurred less than 0.1 seconds ago, and true otherwise.
exercise:7:1.starter:
local lastRequest = {}
local function allowRequest(playerId: number, timestamp: number): boolean
    return false
end
exercise:7:1.harness:
assert(allowRequest(1, 1.0) == true, 'first request allowed')
assert(allowRequest(1, 1.05) == false, 'request after 0.05s throttled')
assert(allowRequest(1, 1.15) == true, 'request after 0.15s allowed')

exercise:7:2.name: JSON Serialization
exercise:7:2.prompt: In Roblox you'd call HttpService:JSONEncode; this sandbox has no engine services, so build the core yourself. Implement packData(data) that serializes a flat table of number values into a JSON object string with keys sorted alphabetically, e.g. {x=10, y=20} -> '{"x":10,"y":20}'.
exercise:7:2.starter:
local function packData(data: { [string]: number }): string
    -- TODO: build '{"key":value,...}' with keys sorted alphabetically
    return ""
end
exercise:7:2.harness:
assert(packData({ x = 10, y = 20 }) == '{"x":10,"y":20}', 'must serialize flat tables')
assert(packData({ b = 2, a = 1 }) == '{"a":1,"b":2}', 'keys must be sorted alphabetically')
assert(packData({}) == '{}', 'an empty table must serialize to {}')

exercise:7:3.name: GUID Wrapping
exercise:7:3.prompt: Engine identifiers arrive raw; transaction systems wrap and normalize them. Using the provided nextGuid() source (in Roblox: HttpService:GenerateGUID(false)), implement getTransactionKey() returning the next GUID wrapped in curly braces and uppercased, e.g. '{GUID-1}'.
exercise:7:3.starter:
-- provided: a raw GUID source (stand-in for HttpService:GenerateGUID)
local __counter = 0
local function nextGuid(): string
    __counter += 1
    return "guid-" .. __counter
end

local function getTransactionKey(): string
    -- TODO: wrap nextGuid() in curly braces, uppercased
    return ""
end
exercise:7:3.harness:
local k1 = getTransactionKey()
local k2 = getTransactionKey()
assert(string.sub(k1, 1, 1) == '{' and string.sub(k1, -1) == '}', 'key must be wrapped in braces')
assert(k1 == string.upper(k1), 'key must be uppercase')
assert(#k1 > 2, 'braces must wrap a real GUID, not be empty')
assert(k1 ~= k2, 'each transaction key must be unique')

exercise:8:0.name: Actor State Protection
exercise:8:0.prompt: Compute the sum of elements in an array without modifying any global tables (such as _G) to ensure the operation remains thread-safe.
exercise:8:0.starter:
local function parallelSafeCompute(t: { number }): number
    return 0
end

exercise:8:1.name: Isolated Random State
exercise:8:1.prompt: The global math.random state is shared across the whole VM — parallel-safe code needs per-actor generators (in Roblox you would reach for Random.new; this sandbox is pure Luau, so build the isolation yourself). Implement makeRandom(seed) returning a generator function: each call advances state = (state * 48271) % 2147483647 and returns state / 2147483647.
exercise:8:1.starter:
local function makeRandom(seed: number)
    -- TODO: keep the state in a closure and advance it on every call
    return function() return 0 end
end
exercise:8:1.harness:
local r1 = makeRandom(42)
local r2 = makeRandom(42)
local r3 = makeRandom(7)
local a, b = r1(), r1()
assert(a >= 0 and a < 1, 'values must be in [0,1)')
assert(a ~= b, 'successive calls must advance the internal state')
assert(r2() == a, 'the same seed must reproduce the same sequence')
assert(r3() ~= a, 'different seeds must produce different sequences')

exercise:8:2.name: Metatable Protection
exercise:8:2.prompt: Protect a table's metatable with the standard __metatable field so getmetatable returns a public sentinel string instead of exposing the real metatable.
exercise:8:2.starter:
local function protectMetatable(t: any)
    -- Protect metatable here
end
exercise:8:2.harness:
local t = {}
protectMetatable(t)
local meta = getmetatable(t)
assert(typeof(meta) == 'string', 'metatable lookup must return the public sentinel string')

exercise:8:3.name: Vector Math Optimizations
exercise:8:3.prompt: Calculate the magnitude of a 3D vector represented as a dictionary {X, Y, Z} using standard math.sqrt.
exercise:8:3.starter:
local function vectorMagnitude(v: { X: number, Y: number, Z: number }): number
    return 0
end

exercise:9:0.name: Scale Coordinate Alignment
exercise:9:0.prompt: Create an object size boundary occupying exactly 80% of width and 60% of height using UDim2.new. Mock a UDim2 struct or use UDim2.new.
exercise:9:0.starter:
local UDim2 = {} -- Mock if needed
function UDim2.new(xs, xo, ys, yo)
    return { Width = { Scale = xs }, Height = { Scale = ys } }
end
local function getMenuSize(): any
    return nil
end
exercise:9:0.harness:
local size = getMenuSize()
assert(size.Width.Scale == 0.8, 'width scale should be 0.8')
assert(size.Height.Scale == 0.6, 'height scale should be 0.6')

exercise:9:1.name: TweenInfo Setup
exercise:9:1.prompt: Construct a TweenInfo-like dictionary with fields Time = 0.2, EasingStyle = 'Bounce', and EasingDirection = 'Out'.
exercise:9:1.harness:
local info = getButtonTweenInfo()
assert(info.Time == 0.2, 'duration should be 0.2')
assert(info.EasingStyle == 'Bounce', 'style should be Bounce')
assert(info.EasingDirection == 'Out', 'direction should be Out')

exercise:9:2.name: Linear Interpolation (Lerp)
exercise:9:2.prompt: Implement a linear interpolation function lerp(startVal, endVal, alpha) that returns the value at progress fraction alpha.
exercise:9:2.starter:
local function lerp(startVal: number, endVal: number, alpha: number): number
    return startVal
end
exercise:9:2.harness:
assert(lerp(10, 20, 0.5) == 15, 'lerp middle point must be 15')
assert(lerp(0, 100, 0.1) == 10, 'lerp 10% point must be 10')

exercise:9:3.name: Mobile Safe Target Offset
exercise:9:3.prompt: Return a mock Size offset structure whose Width.Offset and Height.Offset both meet this course's 44-pixel minimum touch-target heuristic. In a real Roblox UI, also test the physical result across devices and input modes.
exercise:9:3.starter:
local function getMobileButtonSize(): any
    return nil
end
exercise:9:3.harness:
local size = getMobileButtonSize()
assert(size.Width.Offset >= 44, 'width offset must be at least 44')
assert(size.Height.Offset >= 44, 'height offset must be at least 44')

exercise:10:0.name: Plot Class Metatable
exercise:10:0.prompt: Complete the Plot class constructor to bind the new instances to the class template using setmetatable with an __index lookup.
exercise:10:0.harness:
local p = Plot.new()
assert(getmetatable(p) ~= nil, 'plot must have a metatable')
assert(getmetatable(p).__index == Plot, '__index must point to Plot class table')

exercise:10:1.name: Server Purchase Validation
exercise:10:1.prompt: Implement validateAndPurchase(gold, price) to return success (boolean) and remaining gold (number). Gold is only deducted if gold >= price.
exercise:10:1.starter:
local function validateAndPurchase(playerGold: number, itemPrice: number): (boolean, number)
    return false, playerGold
end
exercise:10:1.harness:
local ok, remaining = validateAndPurchase(100, 40)
assert(ok == true and remaining == 60, 'valid purchase should succeed')
local ok2, remaining2 = validateAndPurchase(30, 45)
assert(ok2 == false and remaining2 == 30, 'invalid purchase should be denied')

exercise:10:2.name: State Serialization
exercise:10:2.prompt: Extract the names of purchases from a list of objects { { Name: string, Level: number } } and return them in a flat array of strings.
exercise:10:2.harness:
local data = { { Name = 'Wall1', Level = 1 }, { Name = 'Wall2', Level = 2 } }
local serialized = serializePurchases(data)
assert(serialized[1] == 'Wall1' and serialized[2] == 'Wall2', 'should serialize to list of strings')
local serialized2 = serializePurchases({ { Name = 'Tower', Level = 5 } })
assert(#serialized2 == 1 and serialized2[1] == 'Tower', 'should serialize singular item')

missions.title: Responsive Shop HUD
missions.summary: Build a typed, responsive Roblox shop surface with real state and interaction.
missions.deliverable: A desktop/mobile HUD with a controlled purchase interaction.
acceptance: Define a typed view model and keep UI state behind a ModuleScript boundary.
Build the layout with constraints and touch targets that work at desktop and phone sizes.
Render price, ownership, loading, success, and rejected-purchase states from data.
Wire one controlled purchase request and prevent repeated input while it is pending.
Run desktop and mobile emulator passes and record that no control clips or becomes unreachable.

missions.title: Secure Multiplayer Round
missions.summary: Build an authoritative two-player loop with remotes, lifecycle cleanup, and abuse resistance.
missions.deliverable: A two-player Studio test where invalid client requests are rejected.
acceptance: Write the remote contract: request shape, accepted response, and rejected response.
Validate type, range, ownership, game state, and request rate on the server.
Keep round state authoritative and clean up every signal when the round ends.
Run a two-player Studio test that completes one valid round action on both clients.
Send malformed and spammed requests and confirm neither can mutate authoritative state.

missions.title: Persistent Tycoon Slice
missions.summary: Connect your whole learning path into one small, playable, persistent experience.
missions.deliverable: A vertical slice that survives rejoin and keeps the server authoritative.
acceptance: Model one plot, one currency, and one upgrade behind a server-owned player state object.
Validate a purchase request before changing currency, ownership, or world Instances.
Save and load the minimum persistent state with failure handling and bounded retries.
Bind the HUD to replicated server results instead of trusting client-side economy values.
Pass rejoin, invalid-remote, duplicate-connection, and two-player ownership tests.
